title: Trust Collapse in a Community
description: How a single betrayal propagates through a trust network — and why recovery is asymmetric to the original damage.

initial_trust.label: Initial Trust Level
initial_trust.description: How trusting the community starts (high = more vulnerable to cascade)

transparency.label: Transparency
transparency.description: How quickly betrayal information spreads (higher = faster collapse but faster recovery)

narration.text: A high-trust community (green = trusting). One betrayal — a scam, a lie, a breach of confidence — starts spreading information about the untrustworthy actor.
Distrust spreads faster than the original trust was built. Trust requires consistent positive experience. Distrust requires only one data point.
People withdraw from the community (grey). Not because they were personally betrayed — but because uncertainty about who to trust makes engagement feel risky.
The community is fractured. Cooperation has fallen. This is the economic cost of low-trust environments — every transaction now requires verification, contract, and caution.
Slow recovery begins. Trust rebuilds through consistent demonstrated reliability — much slower than collapse. Nassim Taleb: trust is fragile, distrust is sticky.
Robert Putnam's research: communities with higher social trust have better health outcomes, economic growth, and civic participation. Trust is infrastructure.
